sandy Tayl-or
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Dear Sandy:
This will confirm our telephone conversation on May 10,
1990.
I described the following facts. 
My client is a candidate
for office. 
My clientts controLLed carnpaign committee is
registered with the Secretary of Statets office. 
on the day
that v/e have talked, rny clientrs campaign committee
representative received a tefephone call from a person that had
previously contributed a maxirnum $1,000 contribution to the
committee during this fiscat year. The contributor is a
jeweler. The contributor advised the committee representative
that, on the prior day, he had disseminated advertisements
requesting peopLe to (1) buy a discounted watch from the jeweler
contaj-ning a photo likeness of the candidate and (2) encouraging
such buyers to simul-taneously contribute the amount of the
discount to the candidate's committee. For example, if the
$/atches each cost $500 norrna1ly, the jeweler might provide a
dj-scounted price of $250 and encourage the buyers to contribute
the remaining $250 to the candidate committee.
It is my understanding that the jeweler was not reguiring
that such contributions be made as condition as buying the watch
and that his decision to engage in this activity was nade by
hin, without any prior communication, consuftatlon, or
coorporation with the candidate or the candidaters committee
representatives . It is further ny understanding that he did not
receive the photograph from the canpaign for this purpose,
although he may have received such photo as part of a campaign
brochure or possibly a Christmas card that may have been maited
in the past. In any event, it was his decision to appropriate
the use of the photograph, however obtained, and to promote the
sale of the discounted watches as a method of encouraging
contri,butions to be made to the candi.daters cornmittee.


Sandy TayIor
May 15, 1990
Page Two
Based on this inforrnation, and after checking with others in
your office, you advised me that the activitj-es of the jeweler
would constitute independent expenditures on behalf of the
candidaters candidacy. The independence of these expenditures
was not tainted by the telephone cal-1, to the conmittee
representative after the activity vras coramenced. You also
concurred that the independence of the expenditures would not be
tainted if the committee representative were to again speak with
the jeweler, advise hirn that the independence of the
expenditures coul-d only be rnaintained if there is no
consultation between the entities as to the expenditures, while
at the same tirne encouraging the jeweler to contact your office
for further advice regarding ihe reportability of the
expenditures.
Thank you very much for your prompt response to my
question.
very truly yours,
'fu,"-A
Peter A.
PAB/map
